content.full_text:
FP

May 12, 90 '90

STATE OF WASHINGTON

PUBLIC DISCLOSURE COMMISSION
403 Evergreen Plaza, Mail Stop FJ-42 • Olympia, Washington 98504-3342 • (206) 753-1111 • FAX: (206) 753-1112

May 2, 1990

Mr. John H. Larson, Chairman
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804-0807

Dear Mr. Larson:

A couple of weeks ago, at the suggestion of the chairman of our
Commission, Mr. Bruce Wilson, I talked by telephone with a member
of your staff to learn if an issue that has confronted us in each
of the last two elections has ever been a subject of concern to
your agency as well. He said he'd look into it. Having not
heard back, I'm interpreting that as a response saying you
haven't. Because it is an issue that we're certain we'll have
to deal with again, and something you might face, too, I'd like
to alert you to it.

The issue is simply this: Is the free time given by a broadcaster
to the advocates for one side of a ballot issue because they say
they're without resources to buy time to present their message a
"contribution" by the broadcaster and subject to the reporting
and/or limitation provisions of state campaign finance laws?

Enclosed is a copy of a letter (with referenced attachments) we
sent to the Federal Communications Commission a few months ago
seeking their comments on the matter. To date, we've had no
response, not even an acknowledgement of receipt.

Mr. Wilson and I would be very interested in your comments on the
issue. We're both surprised that it has not, apparently, been
something you've had to address.

I'm looking forward to meeting you in Anchorage in September.

Sincerely,

Graham E. Johnson
Executive Director

"The public's right to know of the financing of political campaigns and lobbying
and the financial affairs of elected officials and candidates far outweighs
any right that these matters remain secret and private."

RCW 42.17.010 (10)

STATE OF WASHINGTON

PUBLIC DISCLOSURE COMMISSION
403 Evergreen Plaza, Mail Stop FJ-42 • Olympia, Washington 98504-3342 • (206) 753-1111 • FAX: (206) 753-1112

February 9, 1990


Mr. Milt Gross
Chief, Fairness/Political Programming
Branch
Federal Communications Commission
1919 "M" Street, N.W.
Washington, DC 20554

Dear Mr. Gross:

The members of the Public Disclosure Commission (PDC), an agency
of Washington State government, have asked that I write you on
their behalf seeking clarification of the Federal Communications
Commission's (FCC) current position on several issues arising
from the Fairness Doctrine. But first, a brief summary of the
background of this inquiry, to put it into context.

A provision of the Washington State public disclosure law (copy
enclosed), which we administer and enforce, prohibits making
contributions in excess of $5,000 to any political campaign
during the three weeks preceding a general election (RCW
42.17.105(8)). It has been alleged in connection with the past
two general elections in Washington (1988 and 1989) that this law
was violated by broadcasters who, having committed to sell
political advertising time to persons advocating a particular
vote on a statewide ballot issue, aired, free of charge,
political advertisements prepared by those advocating an opposite
position. In each case, value of the gratis time would exceed
the $5,000 limit.

In 1988, the PDC issued its Declaratory Ruling No. 5 (copy
enclosed). In that ruling, the Commission said that a
broadcaster's decision to provide the free time was not a
"contribution", as that term is defined in RCW 42.17.020(10).

Just prior to the 1989 general election, in response to a request
from the Honorable Joe King, Speaker of the Washington State
House of Representatives, the PDC revisited this issue. The
Commission concluded that there was no new information presented
that would justify a change in the position expressed in its
Declaratory Ruling No. 5. I conveyed that message to Speaker

"The public's right to know of the financing of political campaigns and lobbying
and the financial affairs of elected officials and candidates far outweighs
any right that these matters remain secret and private."

RCW 42.17.010 (10)

Mr. Gross
February 9, 1990
Page 2

King in a letter dated November 30, 1989 (copy enclosed).

In light of the past two years' experience, the PDC expects the
issue to arise again in the context of future ballot issue
campaigns. As one our commissioners put it, "Requesting free
time late in the campaign seems to be the new game in town."

Because of the nature of the political process, these issues
surfaced during the last days of the campaigns, requiring the PDC
to deal with the questions in a hurried manner, and in an
emotionally charged environment. To allow the PDC to review the
position it has taken well in advance of the 1990 elections, we
need your help. We would very much appreciate, at your earliest
possible convenience, your response to the questions below.

Specifically, we ask:

(1) Does the Fairness Doctrine still exist, in any form?

(2) If the Fairness Doctrine, or any portion of it, remains in
effect, has the FCC made any specific new decision regarding its
application to ballot issues? We have studied the September 22,
1987, letter from Dennis R. Patrick to the Honorable John D.
Dingell (copy attached). Is it still the position of the FCC
that it will accept, investigate, and resolve complaints that
allege violations of the Fairness Doctrine and/or the Cullman
Doctrine with respect to ballot issues, as indicated in that
letter? If the FCC position has changed, we would appreciate
receiving documents reflecting its new policy.

(3) Should your response to the above questions be affirmative,
we would then ask:

(a) What test(s) or criteria can the PDC use to determine
whether the FCC requirements have been satisfied and whether the
providing of additional time is a "contribution"? As noted in
our Declaratory Ruling No. 5, the issue of "voluntariness" is the
touchstone for determining whether the providing of something of
value is a "contribution."

(b) Under what circumstances must a broadcaster air
political advertising, or other means of presenting contrasting
viewpoints on a ballot issue, without charge? Specifically, a
ballot issue advocate spends all available money on staff
salaries, research, direct mail and non-broadcast media, and an
issue opponent buys broadcast time. The broadcaster determines
that neither the advocate nor any other person known to espouse
this point of view is willing to purchase time. May the
broadcaster reject a request to air the advocate's political
advertisement on the grounds the advocate chose to use its

Mr. Gross
February 9, 1990
Page 3

resources for other purposes? Would the FCC accept and pursue
complaints alleging violations of the Fairness Doctrine and/or
the Cullman Doctrine in this regard?

(c) Does a broadcaster have any obligation under federal
law or regulations to investigate the financial status of a
person requesting gratis time who claims to be unable to purchase
the time to air its political advertisement, or require the person
to disclose its financial status? In the scenario described in
paragraph (b), above, may the broadcaster refuse to air the
advocate's message based on its determination that the advocate
has, or could acquire, the resources needed to purchase the
desired time? Would the FCC pursue alleged violations of the
Fairness Doctrine and/or the Cullman Doctrine in this regard?

As said at the outset, we very much need your assistance as we
prepare ourselves for dealing with what appears to be an emerging
calculated campaign strategy -- "the new games in town." Your
prompt attention to our request will be very much appreciated.
Should our questions need to be restated in a way that better
facilitates a response, please let me know.

Sincerely,

Graham E. Johnson
Executive Director

cc: Members, Public Disclosure Commission
The Honorable Joe King

⊗

'89 JAN 20 AUG '01

STATE OF WASHINGTON

PUBLIC DISCLOSURE COMMISSION

403 Evergreen Plaza, Mail Stop FJ-42 • Olympia, Washington 98504-3142 • (206) 753-1111 • FAX: (206) 753-1112


DECLARATORY RULING NO. 5


INITIATIVE CAMPAIGN RECEIVING FREE AIR TIME
UNDER FAIRNESS DOCTRINE (RCW 42.17.020(10);
WAC 390-05-210): A contribution does not
result from a good faith decision by a
broadcaster to provide free air time to an
initiative campaign when advised by counsel
that the "Fairness Doctrine" requires that
action (December 13, 1988).


The Honorable Ruth Fisher
Representative, 27th District
513 North E Street
Tacoma, WA 98403

Michael E. Kipling
Attorney for Fisher Broadcasting, Inc.
Graham & Dunn, Attorneys at Law
34th Floor, Rainier Bank Tower
1301 Fifth Avenue
Seattle, WA 98101-2653


Dear Ms. Fisher and Mr. Kipling:

You petitioned for a declaratory ruling under RCW 34.04.080
and WAC 390-12-250 regarding whether free broadcasting time which
is provided by television and radio broadcasters pursuant to the
"Fairness Doctrine" adopted by the Federal Communications
Commission (FCC) is a "contribution" as defined by RCW
42.17.020(10). At our regular meeting held on October 25, 1988,
Commissioners Beck, McGough and Struthers orally issued a binding
declaratory ruling that such free broadcasting time is not a
"contribution" under RCW 42.17.020(10). It was also decided to
issue this written ruling so as to permit Commissioners Proctor

Declaratory Ruling: 1

"The public's right to know of the finances of political campaigns and lobbying
and the financial affairs of elected officials and candidates far outweighs
any right that these matters remain secret and private."

RCW 42.17.010 (10)

and Wilson an opportunity to review the matter and to provide
guidance for broadcasters and others in the future.

     Your question concerns our interpretation of RCW
42.17.020(10) which reads:

     (10) "Contribution" includes a loan, gift, deposit,
subscription, forgiveness of indebtedness, donation,
advance, pledge, payment, transfer of funds between
political committees, or transfer of anything of value
including personal and professional services for less
than full consideration, but does not include interest
on moneys deposited in a political committee's account,
ordinary home hospitality and the rendering of "part-
time" personal services of the sort commonly performed
by volunteer campaign workers, or incidental expenses
personally incurred by volunteer campaign workers not
in excess of twenty-five dollars personally paid for by
the worker. "Part-time" services, for the purposes of
this chapter, means services in addition to regular
full-time employment, or, in the case of an unemployed
person, services not in excess of twenty hours per
week, excluding weekends. For the purposes of this
chapter, contributions other than money or its
equivalents shall be deemed to have a money value
equivalent to the fair market value of the
contribution. Sums paid for tickets to fund-raising
events such as dinners and parties are contributions;
however, the amount of any such contribution may be
reduced for the purpose of complying with the reporting
requirements of this chapter, by the actual cost of
consumables furnished in connection with the purchase
of the tickets, and only the excess over the actual
cost of the consumables shall be deemed a contribution.

                    FACTUAL BACKGROUND

     At the November 8, 1988, general election, the voters will
be asked to choose between two alternative ballot propositions
dealing with the issue of toxic waste clean-up, Initiative 97 and
Alternative Measure 97B. Counsel representing a number of
broadcasters have advised their clients that the election

Declaratory Ruling: 2

campaign involves a "controversial issue of public importance."
The broadcasters have been advised that the Fairness Doctrine of
the FCC requires contrasting viewpoints be presented to the
public. As a result, various broadcasters have agreed to air
political advertising prepared by the supporters of Initiative 97
free of charge. The enforcement of the Fairness Doctrine by the
FCC permits the broadcaster some discretion in determining the
nature and frequency of the free air time. The supporters of
Initiative 97 stated that they have been granted time which
ranges from one minute for every four minutes purchased by their
opponents to one minute for every seven minutes. The fair market
value of such air time will be substantial.

We understand that the broadcasters may select various
alternatives when attempting to satisfy the duty to present
contrasting viewpoints. Here, the broadcasters chose to air
advertisements which had been produced for the political
committee supporting Initiative 97. We do not believe this
Commission should "second-guess" the good faith decision of the
broadcasters.

No person asserted that the decision of the broadcasters was
made in bad faith to hide an intent to benefit Initiative 97. In
fact, several broadcasters had editorialized in favor of
Alternative Measure 98B. The legal advice was given in good
faith without reference to the merits of the alternative ballot
propositions. There is no indication that the free air time was
provided as part of an effort to support or oppose either of the
ballot propositions.

ANALYSIS

Initially, we note that the Commission has recognized that
the drafters of the Public Disclosure Act did not intend that
campaigns report such things as news stories, feature articles or
editorials appearing in the print or electronic media. These are
expressions protected by the First Amendment of the Constitution
which may be chilled by any governmental effort to require their
fair market value to be reported. Campaigns cannot purchase a
news story, feature article or editorial. Such matters may be of
immeasurable benefit (or harm) to a campaign; however, they have
never been considered to be contributions. SEE WAC 390-16-206.

Declaratory Ruling: 3

The statute defines "contribution" very broadly. Certainly,
it would include a situation where a broadcaster voluntarily
provided free air time to a candidate or a campaign supporting or
opposing a ballot proposition. Similarly, a contribution results
from voluntarily providing air time at less than fair market
value. Those, however, are not the facts before us.

Here, the broadcasters have received legal advice that they
must provide the air time to the supporters of Initiative 97 in
order to comply with the current requirements of the Fairness
Doctrine. The resource is provided under legal compulsion, not
voluntarily.

The statutory definition of a "contribution" necessarily
implies an intent to voluntarily provide resources to an election
campaign at less than fair market value. One of the basic
purposes of requiring disclosure of contributions is to permit
the public to identify those persons supporting election
campaigns. Here, the resource (free air time) is not being
provided with intent to support the Initiative 97 election
campaign.¹ Showing the broadcasters as contributors and, at
least implying their support for that campaign would be
misleading.

It is essential to our decision that the decisions of the
broadcasters are made in good faith. Here, no one contends that
the broadcasters are asserting the Fairness Doctrine as a shield
to hide their true intent to benefit the Initiative 97 campaign.
The broadcasters have received legal advice that they are
required to provide free air time. Our decision is grounded upon
these facts. We would carefully examine any assertion that the

___________________________

¹ We have asked the Initiative 97 campaign to voluntarily
indicate in its reports the value of the air time which is
provided. We believe the public should be aware that this
resource is available so that any comparisons between the
campaigns will more accurately reflect the resources available to
each. At the hearing, the supporters of Initiative 97 indicated
that they would do so.

Declaratory Ruling: 4

decision was made in bad faith as a way to avoid any of the
requirements of the Public Disclosure Act.

The concern of the broadcasters was that if the free air
time was a contribution and if the decision to provide that time
was made during the three weeks before the general election, a
possible violation of RCW 42.17.105(8) would occur. That statute
prohibits giving or receiving a contribution in excess of $5,000
during that twenty-one-day period. We believe, however, that
even if a contribution resulted, this state law prohibition would
be pre-empted by the federal requirements.

As we understand it, the Fairness Doctrine would require the
broadcasters to afford the opposing viewpoint a reasonable
opportunity to present their side of the issue. Here, the legal
advice had been that the Initiative 97 supporters would be
entitled to one-fourth to one-seventh of the air time purchased
by the Initiative 97B supporters. Counsel for the broadcasters
have advised that this is legally required pursuant to federal
regulation promulgated by the FCC.

The United State Supreme Court has recognized that state law
is superceded to the extent that it conflicts with valid
regulations of federal administrative agencies. Public Utilities
Comm'n v. U.S., 333 U.S. 118; Illinois C.R. Co. v. State Pub.
Utilities Comm'n, 245 U.S. 493. Here the state law would
substantially reduce the amount of time which the Fairness
Doctrine would require be afforded. We believe that this
conflict between state law and federal regulation would be
resolved in favor of the federal requirement. This is especially
true when the state law would effectively limit political speech
which a federal agency has determined to be necessary for a
complete discussion of a controversial issue of public
importance.

We recognize that the Fairness Doctrine may be modified or
eliminated by the FCC. In fact, the validity of the doctrine is
now the subject of debate with some arguing that the FCC has
recently abandoned its enforcement of the doctrine in all cases.
Others argue that it only has validity when questions of access
are raised in ballot proposition campaigns. We do not believe
this debate can be resolved by the Public Disclosure Commission.

Declaratory Ruling: 5

We simply do not have the expertise or authority to interpret the
requirements of the FCC.  What is important is that the
broadcasters have made a good faith decision that the Fairness
Doctrine requires them to present contrasting viewpoints on a
controversial issue of public importance, as evidenced by the
fact that they have been advised by counsel.  As the Fairness
Doctrine evolves (or expires), we assume the legal advice will
also evolve.

Therefore, readers should be cautious in applying this
ruling to situations that may arise in the future.

CONCLUSION

We therefore hold that under the circumstances set forth
above, air time provided without charge in advocacy of Initiative
97 does not constitute a contribution within the meaning of RCW
42.17.020(10) because rather than being voluntary, it is required
by the Fairness Doctrine and also because even if this were not
the case, Federal regulations supercede state law when the two
are in conflict.

[signature] David G. Beck
Chairman

[signature]
Commissioner

[signature]
Commissioner

[signature]
Commissioner

[signature]
Commissioner

Attest:

[signature] John S. Johnson
Executive Director
Title

Declaratory Ruling:  6

. - "

STATE OF WASHINGTON

PUBLIC DISCLOSURE COMMISSION
403 Evergreen Plaza, Mail Stop Fj-42 • Olympia, Washington 98504-3342 • (206) 753-1111 • FAX: (206) 753-1112

November 30, 1989

The Honorable Joe King, Speaker
House of Representatives
Legislative Building
Olympia, WA 98504

Dear Speaker King:

The members of the Public Disclosure Commission considered your
letter of November 2, 1989, during their November 21st meeting.
They concluded that the circumstances which existed a year ago,
and which were the basis for our Declaratory Ruling No. 5, remain
unchanged. There is, therefore, no basis for altering the
position taken in that Ruling.

The Commission sees this as largely a matter to be resolved
between the broadcasters and the Federal Communications
Commission. Certain actions by the FCC have, apparently,
eliminated the application of the Fairness Doctrine to some
political expressions, but it is evident that the FCC continues
to hold broadcasters responsible for compliance with the Doctrine
as it pertains to ballot propositions.

I am enclosing a copy of a memorandum I wrote summarizing my
conversation with the management of three Spokane television
stations. You can see that there are differences of opinion
within the broadcast community as to when and where the Fairness
Doctrine applies, and how broadcasters should meet their
obligations under it.

The members of the Public Disclosure Commission said they will
write the FCC asking them to more clearly state the parameters of
the Doctrine so that current confusion about it can be
eliminated.

Your letter was written because of concerns over the actions of
CLOUT, a political committee expressing opposition to Initiative
102. You say it, "the major supporters of CLOUT clearly have the
resources to purchase whatever air time they wish, should they
feel that their viewpoint has not been heard." I have two
observation to share with you concerning that.

First, we have been told by attorneys for broadcasters that

"The public's right to know of the financing of political campaigns and lobbying
and the financial affairs of elected officials and candidates far outweighs
any right that these matters remain secret and private."

RCW 42.17.010 (10)

Speaker Joe King
November 30, 1989
Page 2

Federal courts have said that·broadcasters don't have to look
beyond the current resources of the entity requesting the air
time. It is not the role of broadcasters to speculate as to
whether the entity could get the funds for purchased time, or to
consider a request in light of what they've done with the money
they had.

Second, because of the limitation in RCW 42.17.105(8), a
political committee (CLOUT, in this instance) could not accept
more money from its contributors during the three weeks preceding
election day.

As one of the members of the Public Disclosure Commission said
during the discussion, requesting free time "...is the new game
in town."  It's quite likely that we will see more, not less, of
the practice unless, and until, the Federal Communication
Commission addresses the matter.

Sincerely,

Graham E. Johnson
Graham E. Johnson
Executive Director

cc: Members, Public Disclosure Commission
    Jeff Davis, CLOUT

are in conflict.

Should our questions need to be restated in a way that better
facilitates a responser please 1et me know.

DECLARATORY RULING NO. 5
INITIATIVE CAMPAIGN RECEIVING FREE AIR TIME
UNDER FATRNESS DOCTRTNE (RCW 42.L7.020(10);
WAC 390-05-210): A contribution does not
result 
frcrn a good faith 
decision 
by a
broadcaster to provide free air time to an
initiative 
campaign when advised by counsel
that the "Fairness Doctrine" reguires that
action (December 13, L98B).
The Honorable Ruth Fisher
Representative, 27Lh District
5I3 North E Street
Tacoma, WA 98403
Michael E. Kipling 
I
Attorney for Fisher Broadcasting, Inc.
Graham & Dunn, Attorneys at Law
34th F1oor, Rainier Bank Tower
1301 Fifth Avenue
SeatLle, WA 9810I-2653
Dear Ms. Fisher and Mr. Kipling:
You petitiorred for a declaratory ruling under RCW 34.04.080
and WAC 390-I2-250 regarding whether free broadcasting time which
is provided by television and radio broadcasters pursuant to the
"Fairness Doctrine" adopted by the Federal Communications
Commission (FCC) is a "contribution" 
as defined 
by RCW
42.L7.O20(10). At our regular meeting hetd on October 25, I9BB,
Commissioners Beck, McGough and Struthers ora11y issued a binding
declaratory ruling that such free broadcasting time is not a
"contribution" under RCI,I 42.17.020(10). rt was arso decided to
issue this writEen ruling so as Eo permit Commissioners proctor
Declaratory Ruling:
Tlrc public's right to
ancl lhe iinancial
any right
know of the [intnciry; of politit..tl c,rtnltaigns atrl lobbying
allairs of electr:d ol'(icials antl c,rntlk!ttt,s l,u- outweiglrs
lhnt lhese matters renttin secrt,t antl priv.tte."
.u(-ii,.:
RCW -r2 t7.0 t0 ( to)


and Wilson an opportunity to review the matter and to provide
guidance for broadcasters and others in the future.
You r quest ion conce r ns our interpretation 
of RCW
42.17 .020 (10 ) which reads:
(10 ) "Contribution" includes a loan, gift, deposit,
subscription, forgiveness of indebtedness, donation,
advance, pledger patment, transfer of funds between
political co[unittees, or transfer of anything oi value,
including personal and professional services for less
than fu11 consideration, but does not include interest
on moneys deposited in a political conmittee's account,
ordinary home hospitality and the rendering of "part-
time" personal services of the sort conunonly performed
by volunteer campaign vJorkers, or incidenbal expenses
personally incurred by volunteer campaign r,rorkers not
in excess of twenty-five dollars personally paid for by
the vrorker. "Part-time" services, for the purposes of
this chapter, means services in addition to regular
futl-time employment, or, in the case of an unemployed
person, serviqes not in excess of twenty hours per
week, excluding weekends. For the purposes of this
chapter, cont r ibut ions other than money or its
equivalents shalI be deemed to have a money value
equivalent 
to the fair 
market value of the
contribution. Sums paid for tickets Lo fund-raising
events such as dinners and parties are contributionsi
hor^/ever, the amount of any such contribution may be
reduced for the purpose of complying with the reporting
requirements of this chapter, by the actual cost of
consumables furnished in connection with the purchase
of the tickets, and only the excess over the actual
cost of the consumables shal1 be deemed a contribution.
TACTUAL BACKGROUND
At the November 8, 1988r !€rl€rdI election, the voters will
be asked to choose between two alternative baIlot propositions
dealing with the issue of toxic waste clean-up, Initiative 97 and
Alternative Measure 978. Counsel- representing a number of
broadcasters have advised thei r cl ients that the e tect i on
Declaratory Ruling:


canpaign involves a "controversial issue of pubtic importance.,,
The broadcasters have been advised that the Fairness 6octrine of
the FCC requires contrasting viewpoints be presented to the
public. 
As a result, various broidcasters [rave agreed to air
political advertising prepared by the supporters of I;itiative 
97
free of .charge. The enforcement of the Fairness Doctrine by the
FCC permits the broadcaster some discrebion in determining tfre
lalufe and frequency of the free air time. The supporteis of
Initiative 
97 stated that they have been granted time which
ralrges from one minute for every four minutes purchased by their
opponents to one minute for every seven minutes. The fair market
value of such air time will be substantiat.
We understand that the broadcasters may select various
alternatives 
when attempting to satisfy the duty to present
contrasting viewpoints. Here, the broadcasters chose to air
advertisements which had been produced for the political
committee supporting Initiative 
97. We do not believe this
Commission should "second-guess,, the good faith decislon of the
broadcasters.
No person asser\ed that the decision of the broadcasters lras
made in bad faith to hide an intenb to benefit Initiative 97. In
fact, several broadcasters had editorialized 
in favor of
Alternative Measure 978. The Iegal advice was given in good
faith without reference to the merits of the alteinative ba11ot
propositions. There is no indication that the free air time was
provided as part of an effort to support or oppose either of the
bal- Iot propositions.
sections.analysis:
InitialIy, 
r.re note that. Ehe Commission has recognized that
the drafters of the public Disclosure Act did not intend that
campaigns report such things as ne$rs stories, feature articles or
editorials appearing in the print or eLectronic media. These are
expressions protected by the FirsL Amendment of the Constitution
which may be chilled by any governmental effort to require their
fair market val-ue to be reported. Campaigns cannot !urchase a
neers story, feature article or editorial. 
Such matters may be of
immeasurable benefit (or harm) to a campaign; however, the-y have
never been considered to be contributions. SEE WAC 390-I6-206.
Decl-a ratory RuIing:


The statute defines "contribution" very broadly. Certainly,
it would include a situation where a broadcaster vol,untarily
provided free air time to a candidate or a campaign supporting or
opposing a ballot proposition. Similarly, a contribution results
from voluntarily providing air time at Iess than fair market
value. Those, however, are not the facts before us.
Here, the broadcasters have received IegaI advice that they
must provide the air time to the supporters of fnitiative 97 in
order to comply with the current requirements of the Fairness
Doctrine. The resource is provided under legaI compulsion, not
volunta r i 1y.
The statutory definition of a "contribution" necessarily
implies an intent to voluntarily provide resources to an election
campaign at less than fair narket va1ue. one of the basic
purposes of requiring disclosure of contributions is to permit
the public to i dent i fy those persons suppor t i ng election
campaigns. Here, the resource (free air time) is not being
provided .,wi hh intent to support the Initiative 
97 election
campaign.- Showing the broadcasters as contributors and, at
least inplying th,eir support for that campaign would be
misleading.
It is essential to our decision that the decisions of the
broadcasters are made in good faith. Ilere, no one contends that
the broadcasters are asserting the Fairness Doctrine as a shield
to hide their true intent to benefit the Initiative 97 campaign.
The broadcasters have r ece i ved Iega I advice that they are
required to provide free air time. Our decision is grounded upon
these facts. 
We would carefully examine any assertion that the
I W" hurr" asked the Initiative 
9? campaign to voluntarily
indicate in its reports the value of the air time which ii
provided. We beLieve the public should be aware that this
resource is avai labl e so that any compar isons between the
campaigns wiII more accurately reflect the resources available to
each. At the hearing, the supporters of Initiative 97 indicated
that they would do so.
DeclaraLory Ruling:


decision was made in bad faith as a way to avoid any of the
requirements of the Public Disclosure Act.
The concern of the broadcasters was that if the free air
time lras a contribution and if the decision to provide that time
was made during the three weeks before the general election, 
a
possible violation of RCW 42.L7.105(8) would occur. That statute
prohibits giving or receiving a contribution in excess of 95,000
during that tvrenty-one-day period. 
We believe, hovrever, that
even if a contribution resulted, this state lar,, prohibition would
be pre-empted by the federaL requirements.
As we understand it, the Fairness Doctrine would require the
broadcasters to afford 
the opposing viewpoint a reasonable
opportunity to present Lheir side of the issue. Here, the 1ega1
advice had been that the Initiative 
97 supporters would be
entitled to one-fourth to one-seventh of the air time purchased
by the IniLiative 
97B supporters. Counsel for the broadcasterg
have advised that this is 1ega1ly required pursuanL Lo federal
regulation promulgated by the FCC.
The Unibed Statq Supreme Court has recognized that state law
is supe r ceded to the ex t ent that it conflicts 
wi th valid
regulations of federal administrative agencies. PubIic Utilities
CgUm'n_v._U.S., 333 U.S. tI8, -Lllfnorq_C.R._Co._v. State Pub.
UllffllCC_gomE' n, 245 U.S. 493. Here the state law would
substantially 
reduce the amount of time which the Fairness
Doctrine would require be afforded. 
We believe that, this
conflict 
between state 1aw and federal regulation would be
resolved in favor of the federal requirement. This is especially
true when the state Law would effectively limit political 
speech
which a federal agency has determined to be necessary for 
a
compf e t e discussion 
of a controversial 
issue of pubJ.ic
importance.
We recognize that the Fairness Doctrine may be modified or
eliminated by the FCC. In fact, the validity of the doctrine is
now the subjecE of debate with some arguing that the FCC has
recently abandoned its enforcement of the doctrine in al-1 cases.
Others argue that it only has validity when questions of access
are raised in ballot proposition campaigns. We do not believe
this debate can be resolved by the PubIic Disclosure Commission.
DeclaraLory RuIing:


We simply do not have the expertise or authority to interpret the
requirements of the FCC. What is important is that the
broadcasters have made a good faith decision that the Fairness
DocLrine requires them to present contrasting viewpoints on a
controvers j,al issue of public importance, as evidenced by the
fact that they have been advised by counsel. As the Fairness
Doctrine evofves (or expires), we assume Lhe lega1 advice will
also evolve.
Therefore, readers should be cautious in applying this
ruling to situations that may arise in the future.

CONCLUSION: We therefore hold that under the circumstances set forth
above, air t j-me provided without charge in advocacy of Initiative
97 does not constitute a contribution within Lhe meaning of RC!'I
42.11 .020(10) because rather than being voluntary, it is required
by the Fairness Doctrine and also because even if this vrere not
the case, federal regulations supercede state 1aw when the two
are in conflict.

First, v/e have been tol-d by attorneys for broadcasters that

Federal courts have said that . broadcasters donrt have to fook

embedding.first_500_words: FP May 12, 90 '90 STATE OF WASHINGTON PUBLIC DISCLOSURE COMMISSION 403 Evergreen Plaza, Mail Stop FJ-42 • Olympia, Washington 98504-3342 • (206) 753-1111 • FAX: (206) 753-1112 May 2, 1990 Mr. John H. Larson, Chairman Fair Political Practices Commission 428 J Street, Suite 800 P.O. Box 807 Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 Dear Mr. Larson: A couple of weeks ago, at the suggestion of the chairman of our Commission, Mr. Bruce Wilson, I talked by telephone with a member of your staff to learn if an issue that has confronted us in each of the last two elections has ever been a subject of concern to your agency as well. He said he'd look into it. Having not heard back, I'm interpreting that as a response saying you haven't. Because it is an issue that we're certain we'll have to deal with again, and something you might face, too, I'd like to alert you to it. The issue is simply this: Is the free time given by a broadcaster to the advocates for one side of a ballot issue because they say they're without resources to buy time to present their message a "contribution" by the broadcaster and subject to the reporting and/or limitation provisions of state campaign finance laws? Enclosed is a copy of a letter (with referenced attachments) we sent to the Federal Communications Commission a few months ago seeking their comments on the matter. To date, we've had no response, not even an acknowledgement of receipt. Mr. Wilson and I would be very interested in your comments on the issue. We're both surprised that it has not, apparently, been something you've had to address. I'm looking forward to meeting you in Anchorage in September. Sincerely, Graham E. Johnson Executive Director "The public's right to know of the financing of political campaigns and lobbying and the financial affairs of elected officials and candidates far outweighs any right that these matters remain secret and private." RCW 42.17.010 (10) STATE OF WASHINGTON PUBLIC DISCLOSURE COMMISSION 403 Evergreen Plaza, Mail Stop FJ-42 • Olympia, Washington 98504-3342 • (206) 753-1111 • FAX: (206) 753-1112 February 9, 1990 Mr. Milt Gross Chief, Fairness/Political Programming Branch Federal Communications Commission 1919 "M" Street, N.W. Washington, DC 20554 Dear Mr. Gross: The members of the Public Disclosure Commission (PDC), an agency of Washington State government, have asked that I write you on their behalf seeking clarification of the Federal Communications Commission's (FCC) current position on several issues arising from the Fairness Doctrine. But first, a brief summary of the background of this inquiry, to put it into context. A provision of the Washington State public disclosure law (copy enclosed), which we administer and enforce, prohibits making contributions in excess of $5,000 to any political campaign during the three weeks preceding a general election (RCW 42.17.105(8)). It has been alleged in connection with the past two general elections in Washington (1988 and 1989) that this law was violated by broadcasters who, having committed to sell political advertising time
